text: In this study, we utilize first-principles calculations and deformation potential theory to explore the intrinsic carrier mobility ($\mu$) of monolayer MoS$_2$ sheets and nanoribbons. Unlike graphene, where mobility significantly deteriorates when patterned into nanoribbons, the mobility of armchair MoS$_2$ nanoribbons remains comparable to that of the two-dimensional sheet, despite exhibiting oscillations dependent on ribbon width. Strikingly, we observe a reversal in room-temperature transport polarity: while the MoS$_2$ sheet exhibits hole and electron mobilities of 200.52 and 72.16 cm$^2$V$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$, respectively, a 4 nm nanoribbon switches these values to 49.72 (hole) and 190.89 (electron) cm$^2$V$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$. This robust mobility and the unexpected polarity reversal are driven by the unique characteristics of the inherent edge states in MoS$_2$ nanoribbons. Our findings suggest that combining width confinement with edge engineering offers a powerful strategy for tailoring and enhancing the transport properties of MoS$_2$-based nanostructures.
